era_context: The Roman Empire in its final unified phase under Theodosius I, who died in January 395. The empire is now permanently divided between his sons: Arcadius rules the East (Constantinople) and Honorius the West (Ravenna), each with regents and competing power factions. Barbarian pressure on frontiers intensifies; Christianity is the state religion. In China, the Eastern Jin dynasty faces internal fragmentation and external pressure from Northern Wei.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (archaeological record only)

events.title: Death of Theodosius I and permanent division of the Roman Empire
events.description: Theodosius I died on 17 January 395 CE in Mediolanum (Milan). His will divided the empire between his sons: Arcadius (age 18) inherited the East, Honorius (age 10) the West. This marked the formal, permanent partition of the Roman Empire, though the fiction of unified rule persisted. Arcadius was placed under the guardianship of the praetorian prefect Rufinus; Honorius under the military commander Stilicho. The division created two separate power structures, fiscal systems, and military commands that would never reunify.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae (covers to 378, but context)

events.certainty_note: The date of Theodosius' death (17 January 395) is well-attested in multiple near-contemporary and later sources. The division of the empire and the regency arrangements are documented in ecclesiastical histories and later compilations, though no single contemporary imperial document survives.
cross_references: Leads to 410-001 (Alaric's sack of Rome under Honorius)

events.title: Stilicho assumes effective control of the Western Roman Empire
events.description: Following Theodosius' death, the military strongman Stilicho (half-Vandal, half-Roman) consolidated power as guardian and magister militum (master of soldiers) for the child-emperor Honorius. Stilicho marginalized the Senate and other aristocratic factions, establishing a military autocracy that would dominate the West for the next 16 years. His position was contested by Eastern court factions, particularly Rufinus, creating a rift between the two halves of the empire.

sources.name: Claudian, Panegyric on the Fourth Consulship of Honorius and other poems
Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Stilicho's rise to power is documented in Claudian's contemporary panegyrics and later historical accounts. The consolidation occurred gradually over 395–397, but 395 marks the formal beginning of his regency.

events.title: Arcadius assumes throne of Eastern Roman Empire; Rufinus becomes dominant figure
events.description: Arcadius, age 18, formally became emperor of the East upon Theodosius' death. However, real power lay with the praetorian prefect Rufinus, an ambitious aristocrat who marginalized the young emperor and pursued his own political agenda. Rufinus' dominance created tension with Stilicho in the West and with other Eastern factions, particularly the military establishment and the patriarch of Constantinople.

sources.name: Palladius, Dialogue on the Life of John Chrysostom

events.certainty_note: Arcadius' accession and Rufinus' rise are well-documented in ecclesiastical and secular histories, though written after the fact.

events.title: Visigoths under Alaric begin major incursions into the Balkans
events.description: Following Theodosius' death and the weakening of imperial military coordination, Alaric, a Visigothic leader (possibly already a Roman officer), led a major raid into the Balkans, particularly targeting Moesia and Thrace. This marked the beginning of a sustained period of Visigothic pressure on the Eastern Empire that would culminate in Alaric's march on Constantinople and eventual sack of Rome in 410. The raids exploited the power vacuum created by the division of the empire and the distraction of Eastern and Western courts with internal politics.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: The timing and scale of Alaric's initial incursions are debated. Zosimus places them in 395; other sources suggest they began slightly later. The exact date is approximate, but the general phenomenon is confirmed.
cross_references: Leads to 397-001 (Alaric's formal appointment as magister militum)

events.title: Eastern Jin dynasty faces succession crisis and fragmentation in China
events.description: In 395 CE (or 396 by some sources), the Eastern Jin dynasty in southern China experienced internal instability following the death of Emperor Xiaowu in 396 (or 397). The period saw competing warlords and generals vying for control, with the imperial house becoming increasingly ceremonial. This fragmentation would lead to the eventual collapse of the Eastern Jin and the rise of the Liu Song dynasty in 420. The Northern Wei dynasty, meanwhile, consolidated control over northern China.
key_figures: Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song, rising power)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang
Records of the Eastern Jin (晉書)

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. The dating of specific events in the 390s CE is approximate; sources sometimes differ by 1–2 years. The general political fragmentation is confirmed.

events.title: Council of Constantinople (or synod) addresses ecclesiastical disputes
events.description: In 395 CE, ecclesiastical councils or synods were held in Constantinople to address ongoing Christological and disciplinary disputes. The exact nature and significance of these gatherings is debated by modern scholars, as sources are fragmentary. The period saw increasing imperial involvement in church affairs under Arcadius, with Patriarch Nectarius presiding. These councils reflected the growing power of the patriarchate and the state's interest in doctrinal uniformity.
key_figures: Rufinus (praetorian prefect, influential in church matters)
events.certainty_note: The existence and exact date of ecclesiastical gatherings in Constantinople in 395 are inferred from later ecclesiastical histories. No contemporary records of specific councils survive for this year.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern of imperial church involvement

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of 395 as a clean, permanent 'division' of the empire is complicated by modern scholarship: (1) The division was not intended to be permanent; both emperors and their courts maintained the fiction of a unified empire with shared sovereignty. (2) Stilicho's regency in the West was contested and unstable, not the secure autocracy once portrayed; he faced significant senatorial and military opposition. (3) The dating of Alaric's first incursions is disputed; some scholars place them in 397 or later, not 395. (4) The significance of ecclesiastical councils in 395 is unclear; sources may conflate events from multiple years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 395 CE is rich but heavily shaped by later Christian historiography (Socrates, Orosius, Zosimus writing in the 5th–6th centuries). Pagan sources are sparse; Claudian's panegyrics provide contemporary perspective but are propagandistic. Chinese sources are detailed but compiled centuries later with potential chronological distortions. The Western perspective dominates surviving sources; Eastern and non-Roman perspectives are underrepresented. Archaeological evidence for this year is minimal; the record relies almost entirely on written sources.

graph_edges.note: The division of the empire and weakening of central authority enabled Alaric's eventual sack of Rome
Alaric's raids prompted his formal appointment as magister militum by the Eastern Empire
Stilicho's regency set the stage for his eventual fall and execution
Eastern Jin fragmentation led directly to Liu Song dynasty's founding
